macro_architecture.reasoning: Map-Reduce analysis of 4 containers with 737 total components

layer_structure: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, UI Modules)
Core/Shared Infrastructure Layer (CoreModule, SharedModule, Utility services)

container_analyses.summary: The backend follows a classic layered architecture built on Spring Boot, exposing a rich set of RESTful capabilities for deed registry, archiving, and key‑management operations. While the technical stack (Spring MVC, Data JPA, Security, Scheduler, Lombok) and functional coverage (CRUD, history logging, reporting, scheduled jobs) are solid, the codebase shows moderate separation‑of‑concerns, coupling, and cohesion issues, notably oversized controllers, god‑services, and an over‑populated domain model, resulting in a overall quality grade of C.

capabilities: Create, retrieve, update and delete Deed entries
Log and query Deed entry history
Manage handover data sets and their histories
Handle actions and action streams
Manage document metadata
Perform archiving operations and generate signing tokens
Re‑encrypt data and manage cryptographic state via Key Manager
Expose business purpose information
Run scheduled jobs and background processing
Generate reports and job status information
Health‑check and system monitoring
Provide RESTful API for all above functions

debt_indicators: Potential cyclic dependencies between services and repositories
recommendations: Split oversized controller classes into smaller, feature‑focused controllers.
Introduce interfaces for services and inject them via constructors to reduce tight coupling.
Refactor god services into multiple focused services or use the strategy pattern for interchangeable behaviours.
Review the domain model and consolidate overlapping entities; apply bounded‑context concepts to limit entity proliferation.
Conduct a dependency‑graph analysis to detect and break any cyclic dependencies between services and repositories.
Add a façade or application‑service layer to isolate business logic from REST controllers further.

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The backend delivers comprehensive business functionality on a robust Spring stack, but architectural erosion—manifested as heavy controllers, monolithic services, and an unwieldy domain model—limits maintainability and scalability. Addressing the identified technical debt will elevate code quality and align the implementation with the intended layered design.
top_recommendations: Split oversized controller classes into smaller, feature‑focused controllers.
Introduce interfaces for services and inject them via constructors to reduce tight coupling.
Refactor god services into multiple focused services or use the strategy pattern for interchangeable behaviours.

container_analyses.summary: The frontend container is built on a component‑based Angular architecture that cleanly separates presentation, business, integration, and core/shared layers and leverages a rich stack (TypeScript, RxJS, Angular Material, HttpClient) to expose the Deed Registry & Document Management capabilities via numerous REST adapters. While functional coverage is extensive—supporting deed lifecycle, metadata handling, workflow orchestration, PDF signing, reporting, and authentication—the codebase shows moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion. A large number of services (≈130), adapters (≈50) and pipes (≈67) point to duplicated logic and potential “god” components, resulting in a quality grade of C and notable technical debt.

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, UI Modules)
Core/Shared Infrastructure Layer (CoreModule, SharedModule, Utility services)
technical.integration_style: REST API (HTTP/HTTPS) calls from Angular services to backend endpoints

capabilities: Create, view, edit, approve and delete deeds
Import and export deed data (JSON, bulk capture)
Manage deed metadata and associated documents
Generate, view and sign PDF documents
Handle document archiving and signature validation
Execute and monitor workflow processes (including reencryption, task assignment, job scheduling)
Search, filter and paginate deed entries and related entities
Produce and export reports and analytics
User authentication, authorization and session management
Permission checking and access‑control enforcement
Provide online help and contextual assistance
Display busy indicators, rate limiting and error handling for HTTP calls
Interact with multiple backend REST APIs via adapters
integrations: Xnp Platform REST API (XnpPlatformEndpoint, XnpHelpAdapterService, etc.)

debt_indicators: Excessive number of services (~130) suggests god services and duplicated logic
Large number of adapters (~50) indicates potential lack of unified integration layer
High count of pipes (67) may be used for presentation logic that belongs in components or services
Modules appear many but feature boundaries are not clearly evident from naming
Potential direct use of services inside components reduces abstraction
recommendations: Introduce Facade or Mediator services to group related operations and reduce component-to-service coupling
Consolidate adapters into a smaller, well‑defined Integration layer per external system
Audit and refactor overly large services into domain‑focused services with clear responsibilities
Limit pipe usage to pure view transformations; move complex logic to services or components
Enforce feature‑module boundaries aligning with domain concepts (e.g., Deed, Document, Workflow) to improve cohesion
Add interface abstractions for critical services to enable easier testing and future replacement
Implement linting/architectural rules (e.g., using tslint‑angular or eslint‑plugin‑angular) to enforce separation of concerns

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The frontend provides comprehensive functional coverage but suffers from architectural bloat and moderate quality attributes. Refactoring the service/adaptor landscape and tightening module boundaries will be essential to raise maintainability and scalability.
top_recommendations: Consolidate adapters into a well‑defined integration layer per external system
Refactor large services into domain‑focused facades/mediators to reduce coupling
Enforce clear feature‑module boundaries aligned with domain concepts to improve cohesion

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, UI Modules)
Core/Shared Infrastructure Layer (CoreModule, SharedModule, Utility services)

executive_summary: Map-Reduce analysis completed for 4 containers with 737 components.
top_recommendations: Consider extracting large containers into separate services
Add API documentation for external interfaces
Implement health checks for all containers
